title: Lesson 2: New Identity in Jesus
description: The gospel does not just forgive you. It changes who you are. In this lesson you will discover that your old self was crucified with Christ, that you are a fully adopted child of God clothed in His righteousness, and that freedom and obedience both flow from knowing you are already in the family. Identity comes before behavior. Know who you are, and the rest will follow.

blocks.text: In the last lesson, you encountered God the Father: the one who runs toward you, who delights in you, who loves you before you ever love Him back. That love is where everything begins. But there is a question that follows: if God loves me like that, then who am I? This lesson answers that question. And the answer will change how you see yourself, how you fight sin, and how you walk into every room for the rest of your life.
Made to Reflect
So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them.

You were not just made as an image-bearer. You were made to image: to actively display, reflect, and point people toward who God is. Imagine the glory of God is straight above us, blinding and beautiful, yet people rarely look up to see Him. So God designed humans to be mirrors set at an angle, so that when people look at other people living in step with God, they catch a glimpse of Him. His power, love, and purpose, designed into you, displayed through you. That is your original design.

But you know what happened. When the first humans turned from God, they cracked the mirror. The image was not destroyed, and every person still bears it, but it was distorted. Instead of reflecting God's glory outward, we turned inward, chasing significance through approval, status, control, comfort, and performance. None of those things are big enough to hold the weight of an identity, and they all eventually crack under the pressure.
2.1 - Where in your own life have you looked for significance, approval, or belonging in things that could not deliver it? What did that feel like?
The Old You Is Gone

Here is the part that most people miss about the gospel. When you placed your faith in Jesus, something happened that went far deeper than forgiveness. The old you, the self that was enslaved to sin, defined by the world's approval, and trapped on the performance treadmill, that self was put to death.

I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me. And the life I now live in the flesh I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me.

Paul does not say he was improved or upgraded. He says he was crucified. The old self went into the grave with Jesus, and a new self came out. This is not a makeover but a new creation, and this new self is so deeply connected to Jesus that Paul can say it is no longer merely "I" who lives, but Christ who lives in me. That means your identity is no longer defined by your past, your failures, or your performance record. It is defined by the presence of Jesus living in you.
The Hired Worker's Mistake
In the last lesson you saw the Father run toward his lost son. But there is a detail in that story most people walk right past, and it is the key to understanding your identity.

When the prodigal son was sitting in the pig pen, broke, starving, and covered in shame, he came up with a plan. He rehearsed a speech: "Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Treat me as one of your hired servants" (Luke 15:18-19). Do you see what he was doing? He was preparing to negotiate an employment arrangement. He would come home, not as a son, but as a worker. He would earn his meals, sleep in the servants' quarters, and pay off his debt one day at a time. He believed his failure had disqualified him from the family, so the best he could hope for was a job.

That is the lie most people carry into their relationship with God. They believe they are too far gone to be family, so they settle for being employees. They show up, they perform, they try to earn back what they lost, and they never stop feeling like they owe something. But watch what the Father does. He runs. He embraces. And then he does three things that shatter the hired-worker identity forever.

He puts a robe on the son: not the son's old clothes, not a worker's uniform, but the best robe in the house. He puts a ring on his finger: the family ring, the sign of belonging and authority. And he puts sandals on his feet, because hired workers and slaves went barefoot, but sons wore shoes. The father did not let the son finish his speech. He did not negotiate terms. He clothed him, named him, and restored him, all before the son did a single thing to earn it.
That robe matters more than you might think. It is the same picture Paul paints when he describes what Jesus did for you:
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.

God took your sin and placed it on Jesus, and then He took the perfect righteousness of Jesus and placed it on you. Think of it like the father's robe: when you come before God, you are not standing in your own goodness but wrapped in the goodness, the rightness, and the perfection of Jesus. He is seen, not you. That is not something you earn. It is something you receive, the same way the prodigal received the robe: with empty hands and a stunned heart.
2.2 - Have you been relating to God more like a hired worker trying to earn your place, or like a son or daughter who already belongs? What does that look like in your daily life?
Adopted and Secure
See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are.

John stops to marvel at this. "See what kind of love," as if he is still stunned after years of thinking about it. You are not a forgiven criminal on parole, and you are not a spiritual employee scrapping for God's favor. You are a fully adopted son or daughter with all the rights, belonging, and inheritance that come with it. Your security in this family is not based on your skill set or your track record but on the love of the Father and the finished work of the Son.
And because you are sons, God has sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying, "Abba! Father!" So you are no longer a slave, but a son, and if a son, then an heir through God.

Notice the shift Paul describes: no longer a slave but a son, no longer performing for approval but resting in a relationship. The prodigal son in the pig pen thought the best he could hope for was a job. Paul says God gave you a family, and not just a seat at the table but an inheritance. You do not perform to be included. You are already in.
Free Indeed
There is a verse that would have saved the prodigal son a lot of pain if he had heard it in the pig pen:
So if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed.

Free from what? Free from the lie that you have to earn your way back. Free from the performance record that kept you awake at night. Free from the fear that one more failure will finally disqualify you. The prodigal was sitting in a pig pen planning his worker's contract, and the whole time the father was watching the road, ready to run, ready to restore him as a son. If the son had known he could come home to his family all along, think of how much pain it would have saved him. That is what this verse is telling you: the door is not locked, and it never was. You are free, not because you earned freedom, but because the Son secured it.

This freedom is not permission to live however you want. It is something far better: it is the security to obey from love instead of fear. A hired worker obeys because his paycheck depends on it. A son obeys because his father's name is his name, his father's house is his house, and his father's heart is the safest place he has ever known. The worker asks, "What am I required to do?" The son asks, "Who am I?" and the obedience flows from there.
The Identity Exchange
Here is a simple way to see everything Jesus accomplished for you. Think of what you had before, and what you have now:

Guilty in the Fall, but now Justified in Christ. Your record is sealed by His. Orphan in the Fall, but now Adopted in Christ. You have a Father who chose you. Dead in the Fall, but now Alive in Christ. A new self with a new nature.

This framework is simple enough to draw on a napkin and share with a friend. Justified means your legal standing before God is settled. Adopted means your relational standing in His family is settled. Alive means your spiritual standing as a new creation is settled. All three were accomplished by Jesus, received by faith, and secured forever. These are not goals to achieve; they are realities to believe.
Become What You Are

So if all of this is true, why do we still struggle? Why do we still sin, and why do we still feel like hired workers some mornings? The answer is that the Christian life is learning to live from what is already true about you. You do not obey God to become accepted; you obey because you already are. You do not fight sin to earn a relationship with God; you fight sin because you already have one. The secret to the Christian's battle with sin is not trying harder but believing deeper: believing that you are a son, not a worker, and letting that identity shape every choice you make.
And we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another. For this comes from the Lord who is the Spirit.

The cracked mirror is being restored, one degree of glory at a time. Not because you are straining but because the Source is flowing through you. Identity comes first. Obedience follows. Know who you are, and the rest will come.
2.3 - Where are you still trying to earn what Jesus already secured? What would it look like this week to live as a son or daughter instead of a hired worker?
This Week's Practice

Each morning this week, read John 8:36 slowly: "If the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed." Then ask yourself one question before your day begins: Where am I still living like a hired worker instead of a son? Let that question shape how you walk into your work, your relationships, and your hardest moments this week.

Also this week, read Ephesians 1 in one sitting. Watch for how many times Paul uses the phrase "in Christ" or "in him." Every blessing he describes, chosen, adopted, forgiven, sealed, is yours because of your union with Jesus. You are not trying to get something from God. You already have it. The task is learning to believe it.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
